The tidyverse

The tidyverse refers to two things:

a specific package in R that loads several core packages within the tidyverse.

a specific design philosophy, grammar, and focus on “tidy” data structures developed by Hadley Wickham 1 and the team at Posit (formerly known as R Studio).

You can read the official manifesto here.

The tidyverse package


The core packages within the tidyverse include:

ggplot2 (visualizations)
dplyr (data manipulation)
tidyr (data reshaping)
readr (data import/export)
purrr (iteration)
tibble (modern dataframe)
stringr (text data)
forcats (factors)

The tidyverse philosophy


The principles underlying the tidyverse are:

Reuse existing data structures.
Compose simple functions with the pipe.
Embrace functional programming.
Design for humans.

What is Tidy Data?
 1
Illustrations from the Openscapes blog Tidy Data for reproducibility, efficiency, and collaboration by Julia Lowndes and Allison Horst

section: Why do we Want Tidy Data?
text:
Why do we Want Tidy Data?


Easier to understand many rows than many columns1
Required for plotting in ggplot22
Required for many types of statistical procedures (e.g. hierarchical or mixed effects models)
Fewer issues with missing values and “imbalanced” repeated measures data
Having a consistent method for storing data means it’s easier to learn the tools to work with it since there’s an underlying uniformity.




Most real-world data is not tidy because data are often organized for goals other than analysis (i.e. data entry; not intentionally created as data…) and most people aren’t familiar with the principles of tidy data.


Placing variables in columns also leverages R’s vectorized nature, i.e. most built-in R functions work with values of vectors.In fact, all tidyverse functions are designed to work with tidy data.

Tokenization


In order to create a tidy dataset from lots of text, we need to define a feature that we want to use as our unit of analysis.
This could be many different things (i.e. a sentence, a paragraph, an n-gram, etc.) but the most common token is a word.

Tidying the data

tidy_bob <- clean_bob |> 
  left_join(transcript_data |>
              select(season, episode) |> 
              distinct() |> 
11              mutate(episode_seq = row_number())) |>
12  select(-c(dialogue, raw_text)) |>
13  unnest_tokens(output = word, input = cleaned_text)

tidy_bob

Sentiment Analysis and Tidy Data

Sentiment analysis (aka opinion mining) can be used to get a sense of a source’s attitudes or emotions in a section of writing
We can use pre-existing sentiment dictionaries, create our own, or do a combination of the two
Words can be categorized in a number of ways:

As a simple binary of negative or positive
Along a numeric/likert scale from most negative to most positive
According to a set of particular emotions/attitudes they represent

These measures can then be summarized to represent the overall sentiment or the sentiment within some subset of text (pages, chapters, books, authors, etc.).

tf versus tf-idf




So far we’ve been looking at term frequency (tf)
Another approach is to look at a term’s inverse document frequency (idf), which decreases the weight for commonly used words and increases the weight for words that are not used very much in a collection of documents
This can be combined with term frequency to calculate a term’s tf-idf (the two quantities multiplied together), which is the frequency of a term adjusted for how rarely it is used





library(topicmodels)

season_tf_idf <- season_words |> 
  bind_tf_idf(word, season, n)

Two Approaches







CBOW (Continuous Bag of Words) takes the surrounding words to predict the target word. In contrast, the Skip-gram approach predicts context words based on the given target word.

Creating a Window Function

library(slider)

4slide_windows <- function(tbl, window_size) {
  
5  skipgrams <- slider::slide(
6    tbl,
7    ~.x,
8    .after = window_size - 1,
9    .step = 1,
10    .complete = TRUE
11  )
  
12  safe_mutate <- safely(mutate)
  
13  out <- map2(skipgrams,
              1:length(skipgrams),
14              ~ safe_mutate(.x, window_id = .y))

  out |>
15    list_transpose() |>
16    pluck("result") |>
17    compact() |>
18    bind_rows()
}


4

This function identifies skip-gram windows in order to calculate the skip-gram probabilities

5

Read more about the slide function here

6

Takes in a tibble (of words, in this case)

7

Applies a function to the window; here we’re simply returning the words as-is

8

Defines how many elements after the current one should be included in the window

9

How many elements to shift by when computing the window

10

If TRUE, it only evaluates complete windows (i.e. entire window size is available from the current element)

11

skipgrams is a list-column containing a skipgram window for each word, for each episode

12

Creates a version of mutate that won’t break the function, rather it’ll capture results and errors in a list object for each skipgram

13

map2 mutates over each of these skipgrams to append a window_id column (which is the index of the skipgram for that epidose) so we can identify them when we unpack this extremely nested words list later

14

The function that map2 is iterating through our skipgrams and their index numbers with is safe_mutate, which will create a list of 2 for each skipgram it iterates through, putting successfully mutated skipgram results into result and unsuccessfully mutated skipgram results into error

15

Turns each episode’s words list from a list of skipgrams (each with a list of result and error per our safe_mutate function) into its transposed version, in this case two lists (result and error), each composed of the full list of skipgrams for that episode

16

pluck pulls out the result list for each episode’s words column so now each episode’s list is composed of a list of tibbles for each skipgram widow with their corresponding window_id

17

Removes empty skipgrams (i.e. where window was incomplete)

18

Combines all skipgram words and window_ids into one complete tibble in words column corresponding to each episode

Let’s Have a Look Around 👀

library(widyr)
library(furrr)

19plan(multisession)

tidy_pmi <- nested_eps |> 
20  mutate(words = future_map(words, \(x) slide_windows(x, 4L))) |>
21  unnest(words) |>
22  unite(window_id, episode_seq, window_id) |>
23  pairwise_pmi(word, window_id)


19

Allows this process to be run computationally in parallel (huge time-saver 🙏) since each episode can be run independently of the others

20

Takes the words column and applies our slide_windows() function to each episode’s list with a skip-gram window of 4 words

21

Unnests the words column so it is no longer an embedded list per episode but instead we have a tibble with columns for episode_seq, word, and window_id. Instead of 272 rows (1 row for each episode) we now have 925,280 rows (1 row for each word in a skip-gram window for all 272 episodes)

22

Overwrites the window_id column to be a combination of episode_seq and window_id, separated by _

23

Uses each instance of a word and its associated window_id to calculate the logarithm of probability of finding two words together, normalized by the probability of finding each of the words alone.

Our (slide_windows) function enables us to define a fixed-size moving window around each word. Within those windows we are able to see if two given words are seen together and calculate probabilities based on those occurrences, or lack of occurrences.
tl;dr, tidy_pmi quantifies how often we find each word near each other word.

Singular Value Decomposition (SVD)

We can next determine the word vectors from the PMI values using singular value decomposition (SVD)

This is a data reduction technique akin to PCA (Principle Component Analysis), i.e. it works by taking our data and decomposing it onto special orthogonal axes.

The first axis is chosen to capture as much of the variance as possible
Keeping that first axis fixed, the remaining orthogonal axes are rotated to maximize the variance in the second axis
This is then repeated for all the remaining axes

tidy_word_vectors <- tidy_pmi  |> 
24  widely_svd(
25    item1, item2, pmi,
26    nv = 100,
27    maxit = 1000
  )

tidy_word_vectors


24

Takes our tidy_pmi table, turns it into a wide matrix, and performs dimensionality reduction on it, and returns it in tidy format

25

item1 serves as the item we want to perform dimensionality reduction on, item2 is the feature that links items to one another, and pmi is the value we’re reducing

26

Number of principle components to estimate

27

Optional argument specifying the maximum number of iterations

Pre-trained Embeddings

Word-embeddings work well when you have a large amount of data and our Bob dataset is a bit small

In that case, you may want to explore using pre-trained word-embeddings

We’re going to use the wordsalad package which provides some convenient functions to access some of the more popular pre-trained embeddings



library(wordsalad)

glove_word_vec <- 
29  glove(text = clean_bob$cleaned_text,
        dim = 100, 
        window = 4, 
        min_count = 50, 
        stopwords = stop_words$word, 
        composition = "tibble")

glove_word_vec

Latent Dirichlet allocation (LDA)

A type of Bayesian network model that makes two general assumptions

Each document is a mixture of topics
Each topic is a distribution of words




LDA is a mathematical method for estimating  both of these at the same time: finding the  mixture of words that is associated with each  topic, while also determining the mixture  of topics that describes each document.

Divide Each Book into Sections

by_section <- books |> 
9  filter(text != "") |>
10  mutate(text = str_remove_all(text, "[:digit:]"),
11         word_count = str_count(text, "\\S+")) |>
  group_by(title) |> 
12  mutate(cumulative_words = cumsum(word_count),
13         row_chunk = ceiling(cumulative_words / 2000)) |>
  group_by(title, row_chunk) |> 
14  summarize(section = str_c(text, collapse = " "), .groups = "drop") |>
15  unite(document, title, row_chunk, sep = "_", remove = FALSE) |>
  select(document, section)

Data Packages
R has a big user base. If you are working with a popular data source, it will often have a devoted R package on CRAN or Github.

Examples:

WDI: World Development Indicators (World Bank)
tidycensus: Census and American Community Survey
quantmod: financial data from Yahoo, FRED, Google
gssr: The General Social Survey Cumulative Data (1972-2021)
psidR: Panel Study of Income Dynamics (basic & public datasets)



If you have an actual data file, you’ll have to import it yourself…

Delimited Text Files
Besides a package, it’s easiest when data is stored in a text file. The most commonly encountered delimited file is a .csv.

A comma-separated values (.csv) file looks like the following:

readr
R has some built-in functions for importing data, such as read.table() and read.csv().

The readr package provides similar functions, like read_csv(), that have slightly better features:

Faster!
Better defaults (e.g. doesn’t automatically convert characters to factors)
A bit smarter about dates and times
Loading progress bars for large files



readr is one of the core tidyverse packages so loading tidyverse will load it too:

library(tidyverse)



Alternatively, you can just load readr like so:

library(readr)

Alternate Solution
When you import data from an external file you’ll also see it in the Global Environment tab in the upper-right pane of RStudio:



You can also import the data manually!
In the upper right-hand pane of RStudio (make sure you’re in the Environment tab), select:
Import Dataset > From Text (readr) and browse to the file on your computer1.


Once you’ve imported the data, you can copy/paste the import code from the console into your file!!
This makes the process reproducible!




Ideally you’ve saved it in your project folder!
Manual Data Import

Specifying NAs
Sometimes a particular dataset or file read from a different software will code NAs differently than R. If that’s the case, you can add additional specifications to read_csv for what to read in as NA.

billboard_2000_raw <- read_csv(file = "data/billboard.csv", 
                               na = c("N/A", "999"))

Skipping lines
Depending on how the data were input, there may be several lines that precede the beginning of the data table you’re interested in importing. You can skip these lines of metadata with the skip argument:

billboard_2000_raw <- read_csv(file = "data/billboard.csv", 
                               skip = 1)

Variable names
read_csv will automatically take the first row as column names. If you want to rename them you can save yourself some time recoding later on if you specify your preferred variable names upfront with the col_names argument.

It takes a character vector to be used as column names (in their order of appearance).

billboard_renamed <- read_csv(file = "data/billboard.csv",
                              col_names = c("year", "artist", "track", "time", "date_entered", 
1                                            paste("wk", 1:76, sep = "_")))

2billboard_renamed |>  names() |> head(10)


1

First few entries: “wk_1” “wk_2” “wk_3”

2

names returns the column names.




 [1] "year"         "artist"       "track"        "time"         "date_entered"
 [6] "wk_1"         "wk_2"         "wk_3"         "wk_4"         "wk_5"        




If you don’t have any variable names you can specify that instead.

billboard_2000_raw <- read_csv(file = "data/billboard.csv", 
                               col_names = FALSE)

Snake Case
If you simply want to change your variables to snake case (all lower case; words separated by _), you can use the function clean_names() from the janitor package which replaces other punctuation separators with _.

# Download package first
3# install.packages("janitor")

# Create new object for renamed data
billboard_renamed <- billboard_2000_raw |> 
4  janitor::clean_names(numerals = "right")

billboard_renamed |>  names() |> head(10)


3

Run in the console first.

4

You can call a function without loading its package by specifying its package name followed by :: before it;  The numerals argument specifies if you additionally want to put a separator before a number.






 [1] "year"         "artist"       "track"        "time"         "date_entered"
 [6] "wk_1"         "wk_2"         "wk_3"         "wk_4"         "wk_5"

section: Other Data File Types with readr
text:
Other Data File Types with readr
The other functions in readr employ a similar approach to read_csv so the trick is just knowing which to use for what data type.


read_csv2 is separated by semicolons (instead of commas)
read_tsv is separated by tabs
read_delim guesses the delimiter
read_fwf reads in fixed-width-files
read_table is a variation of fwf where columns are separated by white space
read_log reads in Apache-style log files

section: Other Packages to Read in Data
text:
Other Packages to Read in Data
There are a range of other ways, besides delimited files, that data are stored.
The following packages are part of the extended tidyverse and therefore operate with similar syntax and logic as readr.

section: Other Packages to Read in Data
text:
Other Packages to Read in Data
There are a range of other ways, besides delimited files, that data are stored.
The following packages are part of the extended tidyverse and therefore operate with similar syntax and logic as readr.





For Excel files (.xls or .xlsx), use package readxl1




Note: For Excel files and Googlesheets You won’t keep text formatting, color, comments, or merged cells. See the openxlsx package for those capabilities. Also, tidyxl can help import non-tabular data from Excel.
Functions have additional arguments to read in specific sheets or a range of cells.

section: Most Common Issue with Reading in Data
text:
Most Common Issue with Reading in Data
The most common problem that occurs when reading in data is having mixed data. Most often, given the heuristic provided in the last slide, R will parse a variable as a character string to preserve whatever it contains.

What Went Wrong?
Since readr uses the values in the first 1000 rows to guess the type of the column (logical, numeric, date/date-time, character), if the first 1000 rows don’t have any data, they will be coded as logical variables.

There are not many songs in the data that charted for 60+ weeks—and none in the first 1000 that charted for 66+ weeks!





  NA is logical?

class(c("apple", NA, "mango", "blackberry", "plum")) 
class(c(NA, NA, NA, NA, NA))

class returns the data type of its first argument.

6

as.Date turns a character string of dates into an official date class in Base R. If we had an accompanying time stamp we would need to use as.POSIXct which turns a character string of dates and times into an official date-time class in Base R.

Column types
To specify a default column type you can use .default like so:

billboard_2000_raw <- read_csv(file = "data/billboard.csv", 
                               col_types = cols(.default = col_character())) 



Another useful helper is cols_only() for when you only want to read in a subset of all available variables.

billboard_2000_raw <- read_csv(file = "data/billboard.csv", 
                               col_types = cols_only(x = col_character)) 




In summary, the col_types argument gives you greater control over how your data are read in and can save you recoding time down the road and/or point out where your data are behaving differently than you expect.

Writing Delimited Files
Getting data out of R into a delimited file is very similar to getting it into R:

write_csv(billboard_2000_raw, path = "data/billboard_data.csv")

This saved the data we pulled off the web in a file called billboard_data.csv in the data folder of my working directory.

However, saving data in this way will not preserve R data types since delimited files code everything as a character string.


To save R objects and all associated metadata you have two options:

.Rds format:.Rdata or .Rda format:



Used for single objects, doesn’t save the original object name
Save: write_rds(old_object_name, "path.Rds")
Load: new_object_name <- read_rds("path.Rds")




Used for saving multiple files where the original object names are preserved
Save: save(object1, object2, ... , file = "path.Rdata")
Load: load("path.Rdata") without assignment operator

Death to Spreadsheets
Tools like Excel or Google Sheets let you manipulate spreadsheets using functions.

Spreadsheets are not reproducible: It’s hard to know how someone changed the raw data!
It’s hard to catch mistakes when you use spreadsheets1.


We want to know how to use R to manipulate data more transparently and reproducibly.

Don’t be the next sad Research Assistant who makes headlines with an Excel error! (Reinhart & Rogoff, 2010)

Comparing objects

You may also see && and || but they are what’s known as short-circuiting operators and are not to be used in dplyr functions (used for programming not data manipulation); they’ll only ever return a single TRUE or FALSE.

Logical Summaries




any():
all():





the equivalent of |; it’ll return TRUE if there are any TRUE’s in x
the equivalent of &; it’ll return TRUE only if all values of x are TRUE’s





C <- c(5, 10, NA, 10, 20, NA)
any(C <= 10) 
1all(C <= 20)
2all(C <= 20, na.rm = TRUE)
3mean(C, na.rm = TRUE)

Like other summary functions, they’ll return NA if there are any missing values present and it’s FALSE.

2

Use na.rm = TRUE to remove NAs prior to evaluation.

3

When you evaluate a logical vector numerically, TRUE = 1 and FALSE = 0. This makes sum() and mean() useful when summarizing logical functions (sum gives number of TRUEs and mean gives the proportion).

Conditional transformations

if_else()

If you want to use one value when a condition is TRUE and another value when it’s FALSE.


if_else(condition = "A logical vector", 
        true = "Output when condition is true", 
        false = "Output when condition is false")




x <- c(-3:3, NA)
4if_else(x > 0, "+ve", "-ve", "???")


4

There’s an optional fourth argument, missing which will be used if the input is NA.




[1] "-ve" "-ve" "-ve" "-ve" "+ve" "+ve" "+ve" "???"





case_when()

A very useful extension of if_else() for multiple conditions1.



case_when(
  x == 0   ~ "0",
  x < 0    ~ "-ve", 
  x > 0    ~ "+ve",
5  is.na(x) ~ "???"
6)


5

Use .default if you want to create a “default”/catch all value.

6

Both functions require compatible types: i.e. numerical and logical, strings and factors, dates and datetimes, NA and everything.

Note that if multiple conditions match in case_when(), only the first will be used.

dplyr Basics
All dplyr functions have the following in common:

The first argument is always a data frame.
The subsequent arguments typically describe which columns to operate on, using the variable names (without quotes).
The output is always a new data frame.


Each function operates either on rows, columns, groups, or entire tables.


To save the transformations you’ve made to a data frame you’ll need to save the output to a new object.

14  filter(dep_delay > 120)

Summarizing Data: summarize()
summarize() calculates summaries of variables in your data:

Count the number of rows
Calculate the mean
Calculate the sum
Find the minimum or maximum value


You can use any function inside summarize() that aggregates multiple values into a single value (like sd(), mean(), or max()).

summarize() Example
Let’s see what this looks like in our flights dataset:


flights |> 
  summarize(
28    avg_delay = mean(dep_delay)
  )


28

The NA produced here is a result of calling mean on dep_delay. Any summarizing function will return NA if any of the values are NA. We can set na.rm = TRUE to change this behavior.

section: Select Specific Rows Per Group: slice_*
text:
Select Specific Rows Per Group: slice_*
There are five handy functions that allow you extract specific rows within each group:

df |> slice_head(n = 1) takes the first row from each group.
df |> slice_tail(n = 1) takes the last row in each group.
df |> slice_min(x, n = 1) takes the row with the smallest value of column x.
df |> slice_max(x, n = 1) takes the row with the largest value of column x.
df |> slice_sample(n = 1) takes one random row.


Let’s find the flights that are most delayed upon arrival at each destination.

section: Select Specific Rows Per Group: slice_*
text:
Select Specific Rows Per Group: slice_*

flights |> 
  group_by(dest) |> 
31  slice_max(arr_delay, n = 1) |>
  relocate(dest) 


31

Why Merge Data?
In practice, we often collect data from different sources. To analyze the data, we usually must first combine (merge) them.

For example, imagine you would like to study county-level patterns with respect to age and grocery spending. However, you can only find,

County level age data from the US Census, and
County level grocery spending data from the US Department of Agriculture



Merge the data!!


To do this we’ll be using the various join functions from the dplyr package.

Joining in Concept
We need to think about the following when we want to merge data frames A and B:


Which rows are we keeping from each data frame?




Which columns are we keeping from each data frame?





Which variables determine whether rows match?

Keys
Keys are the way that two datasets are connected to one another. The two types of keys are:

Primary: a variable or set of variables that uniquely identifies each observation.

When more than one variable makes up the primary key it’s called a compound key

Foreign: a variable (or set of variables) that corresponds to a primary key in another table.

Primary Keys 
Let’s look at our data to gain a better sense of what this all means.

Checking Keys
A nice feature of these data are that the primary and foreign keys have the same name and almost every variable name used across multiple tables has the same meaning.1 This isn’t always the case!2

It is good practice to make sure your primary keys actually uniquely identify an observation and that they don’t have any missing values.



planes |> 
1  count(tailnum) |>
  filter(n > 1)


1

If your primary keys uniquely identify each observation you’ll get an empty tibble in return.






# A tibble: 0 × 2
# ℹ 2 variables: tailnum <chr>, n <int>





planes |> 
2  filter(is.na(tailnum))

If none of your primary keys are missing you’ll get an empty tibble in return here too.

Basic (Equi-) Joins
All join functions have the same basic interface: they take a pair of data frames and return one data frame.

The order of the rows and columns is primarily going to be determined by the first data frame.


dplyr has two types of joins: mutating and filtering.




Mutating Joins
Add new variables to one data frame from matching observations from another data frame.

left_join()
right_join()
inner_join()
full_join()




Filtering Joins
Filter observations from one data frame based on whether or not they match an observation in another data frame.

semi_join()
anti-join()
Mutating Joins

left_join() 








The most common type of join
Appends columns from y to x by the rows in x

NA added if there is nothing from y

Natural join: when all variables that appear in both datasets are used as the join key

If the join_by() argument is not specified, left_join() will automatically join by all columns that have names and values in common.

left_join in nycflights13

flights2 <- flights |> 
  select(year, time_hour, origin, dest, tailnum, carrier)

  left_join(airlines)

This type of join is useful for finding missing values that are implicit in the data (i.e. NAs that don’t show up in the data but only exist as an absence.)

More Than One Match


There are three possible outcomes for a row in x:

If it doesn’t match anything, it’s dropped.
If it matches 1 row in y, it’s preserved.
If it matches more than 1 row in y, it’s duplicated once for each match.



What happens if we match on more than one row?

More Than One Match

df1 <- tibble(key = c(1, 2, 2), val_x = c("x1", "x2", "x3"))
df2 <- tibble(key = c(1, 2, 2), val_y = c("y1", "y2", "y3"))

df1 |> 
  inner_join(df2, join_by(key))



Warning in inner_join(df1, df2, join_by(key)): Detected an unexpected many-to-many relationship between `x` and `y`.
ℹ Row 2 of `x` matches multiple rows in `y`.
ℹ Row 2 of `y` matches multiple rows in `x`.
ℹ If a many-to-many relationship is expected, set `relationship =
  "many-to-many"` to silence this warning.

If you’re doing this deliberately, set relationship = “many-to-many”, as the warning suggests.



Given their nature, filtering joins never duplicate rows like mutating joins do. They will only ever return a subset of the datasets.

Non-Equi Joins
The joins we’ve discussed thus far have all been equi-joins, where the rows match if the x key equals the y key. But you can also specify other types of relationships.

dplyr has four different types of non-equi joins:





Cross joins match every pair of rows.







Cross joins, aka self-joins, are useful when generating permutations (e.g. creating every possible combination of values). This comes in handy when creating datasets of predicted probabilities for plotting in ggplot.

Non-Equi Joins
The joins we’ve discussed thus far have all been equi-joins, where the rows match if the x key equals the y key. But you can also specify other types of relationships.
dplyr has four different types of non-equi joins:



Cross joins match every pair of rows.
Inequality joins use <, <=, >, and >= instead of ==.

Overlap joins are a special type of inequality join designed to work with ranges1.







Inequality joins can be used to restrict the cross join so that instead of generating all permutations, we generate all combinations.
Overlap joins provide three helpers that use inequality joins to make it easier to work with intervals: between(), within(), overlaps(). Read more about their functionality and specifications here.

Non-Equi Joins
The joins we’ve discussed thus far have all been equi-joins, where the rows match if the x key equals the y key. But you can also specify other types of relationships.
dplyr has four different types of non-equi joins:



Cross joins match every pair of rows.
Inequality joins use <, <=, >, and >= instead of ==.

Overlap joins are a special type of inequality join designed to work with ranges.

Rolling joins are similar to inequality joins but only find the closest match.






Rolling joins are a special type of inequality join where instead of getting every row that satisfies the inequality, you get just the closest row. You can turn any inequality join into a rolling join by adding closest().

What is Tidy Data
Tidy data1 (aka “long data”) are such that:






The values for a single variable are in their own column.
The values for a single observation are in their own row.
There is only one value per cell.

Read the original article here.

section: Why do we Want Tidy Data?
text:
Why do we Want Tidy Data?

Easier to understand many rows than many columns1
Required for plotting in ggplot22
Required for many types of statistical procedures (e.g. hierarchical or mixed effects models)
Fewer issues with missing values and “imbalanced” repeated measures data
Having a consistent method for storing data means it’s easier to learn the tools to work with it since there’s an underlying uniformity.


Most real-world data is not tidy because data are often organized for goals other than analysis (i.e. data entry) and most people aren’t familiar with the principles of tidy data.

Placing variables in columns also leverages R’s vectorized nature, i.e. most built-in R functions work with values of vectors.In fact, all tidyverse functions are designed to work with tidy data.

tidyr
The tidyr package provides functions to tidy up data.

Key functions:

pivot_longer(): takes a set of columns and pivots them down (“longer”) to make two new columns (which you can name yourself):

A name column that stores the original column names
A value with the values in those original columns





pivot_wider(): inverts pivot_longer() by taking two columns and pivoting them up and across (“wider”) into multiple columns

pivot_longer()
This function usually takes three arguments:

cols: The columns that need to be pivoted (are not variables)
names_to: Names the new variable that is stored in multiple columns
values_to: Names the variable stored in the cell values

billboard_2000 <- billboard_2000_raw |> 
1  pivot_longer(cols = starts_with("wk"),

Pivoting Better: values_drop_na
Adding the argument values_drop_na = TRUE to pivot_longer() will remove rows with missing ranks. Since these NAs don’t really represent unknown observations (i.e. they were forced to exist by the structure of the dataset) this is an appropriate approach here.

billboard_2000 <- billboard_2000_raw %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = wk1:wk76, 
               names_to = "week", 
               values_to = "rank", 
               values_drop_na = TRUE)
summary(billboard_2000$rank)

   1.00   26.00   51.00   51.05   76.00  100.00

parse_number()
The week column is of the type character, but it should be numeric.

[1] "wk1" "wk2" "wk3" "wk4" "wk5" "wk6"



parse_number() grabs just the numeric information from a character string:

billboard_2000 <- billboard_2000 |> 
2    mutate(week = parse_number(week))
summary(billboard_2000$week)

You can use mutate() to overwrite existing columns.

Use pivot_longer arguments
Alternatively (and more efficiently), there are a number of optional arguments for pivot_longer that are meant to help deal with naming issues.


billboard_2000 <- billboard_2000_raw %>%
  pivot_longer(starts_with("wk"), 
               names_to        = "week", 
               values_to       = "rank",
               values_drop_na  = TRUE,
3               names_prefix    = "wk",
4               names_transform = list(week = as.integer))

Multiple Variables in Column Names
A more challenging situation occurs when you have multiple pieces of information crammed into the column names, and you would like to store these in separate new variables.

This dataset contains tuberculosis diagnoses collected by the World Health Organization.

who2



# A tibble: 7,240 × 58
   country      year sp_m_014 sp_m_1524 sp_m_2534 sp_m_3544 sp_m_4554 sp_m_5564
   <chr>       <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>
 1 Afghanistan  1980       NA        NA        NA        NA        NA        NA
 2 Afghanistan  1981       NA        NA        NA        NA        NA        NA
 3 Afghanistan  1982       NA        NA        NA        NA        NA        NA
 4 Afghanistan  1983       NA        NA        NA        NA        NA        NA
 5 Afghanistan  1984       NA        NA        NA        NA        NA        NA
 6 Afghanistan  1985       NA        NA        NA        NA        NA        NA
 7 Afghanistan  1986       NA        NA        NA        NA        NA        NA
 8 Afghanistan  1987       NA        NA        NA        NA        NA        NA
 9 Afghanistan  1988       NA        NA        NA        NA        NA        NA
10 Afghanistan  1989       NA        NA        NA        NA        NA        NA
# ℹ 7,230 more rows
# ℹ 50 more variables: sp_m_65 <dbl>, sp_f_014 <dbl>, sp_f_1524 <dbl>,
#   sp_f_2534 <dbl>, sp_f_3544 <dbl>, sp_f_4554 <dbl>, sp_f_5564 <dbl>,
#   sp_f_65 <dbl>, sn_m_014 <dbl>, sn_m_1524 <dbl>, sn_m_2534 <dbl>,
#   sn_m_3544 <dbl>, sn_m_4554 <dbl>, sn_m_5564 <dbl>, sn_m_65 <dbl>,
#   sn_f_014 <dbl>, sn_f_1524 <dbl>, sn_f_2534 <dbl>, sn_f_3544 <dbl>,
#   sn_f_4554 <dbl>, sn_f_5564 <dbl>, sn_f_65 <dbl>, ep_m_014 <dbl>, …




The first two columns are self explanatory but what’s going on with the rest?

Multiple Variables in Column Names
Data documentation and some minor investigation would lead you to figure out that the three elements in each of these column names are actually data!

The first piece, sp/sn/rel/ep, describes the method used for the diagnosis
The second piece, m/f is the gender (coded as a binary variable in this dataset)
The third piece, 014/1524/2534/3544/4554/5564/65 is the age range (014 represents 0-14, for example)


To organize the six pieces of information in this dataset into six separate columns, we use pivot_longer() with a vector of column names for names_to and instructors for splitting the original variable names into pieces for names_sep as well as a column name for values_to!

Multiple Variables in Column Names

who2 |> 
  pivot_longer(
    cols = !(country:year),
    names_to = c("diagnosis", "gender", "age"), 
5    names_sep = "_",
    values_to = "count"
  )


5

You can use names_pattern instead of names_sep to extract variables from more complicated naming scenarios if you are familiar with regular expressions.

   country      year diagnosis gender age   count
   <chr>       <dbl> <chr>     <chr>  <chr> <dbl>
 1 Afghanistan  1980 sp        m      014      NA
 2 Afghanistan  1980 sp        m      1524     NA

 4 Afghanistan  1980 sp        m      3544     NA
 5 Afghanistan  1980 sp        m      4554     NA
 6 Afghanistan  1980 sp        m      5564     NA
 7 Afghanistan  1980 sp        m      65       NA
 8 Afghanistan  1980 sp        f      014      NA
 9 Afghanistan  1980 sp        f      1524     NA
10 Afghanistan  1980 sp        f      2534     NA
# ℹ 405,430 more rows

Variable & Values in Column Names
This dataset contains data about five families, with the names and dates of birth of up to two children.

household



# A tibble: 5 × 5
  family dob_child1 dob_child2 name_child1 name_child2
   <int> <date>     <date>     <chr>       <chr>      
1      1 1998-11-26 2000-01-29 Susan       Jose       
2      2 1996-06-22 NA         Mark        <NA>       
3      3 2002-07-11 2004-04-05 Sam         Seth       
4      4 2004-10-10 2009-08-27 Craig       Khai       
5      5 2000-12-05 2005-02-28 Parker      Gracie     



The new challenge in this dataset is that the column names contain the names of two variables (dob, name) and the values of another (child, with values 1 or 2).

Variable & Values in Column Names


household |> 
  pivot_longer(
    cols = !family, 
6    names_to = c(".value", "child"),
    names_sep = "_", 
7    values_drop_na = TRUE
  )



6

.value isn’t the name of a variable but a unique value that tells pivot_longer to use the first component of the pivoted column name as a variable name in the output.

7

Using values_drop_na = TRUE again since not every family has 2 children.




# A tibble: 9 × 4
  family child  dob        name  
   <int> <chr>  <date>     <chr> 
1      1 child1 1998-11-26 Susan 
2      1 child2 2000-01-29 Jose  
3      2 child1 1996-06-22 Mark  
4      3 child1 2002-07-11 Sam   
5      3 child2 2004-04-05 Seth  
6      4 child1 2004-10-10 Craig 
7      4 child2 2009-08-27 Khai  
8      5 child1 2000-12-05 Parker
9      5 child2 2005-02-28 Gracie

pivot_wider
pivot_wider() is the opposite of pivot_longer(), which you use if you have data for the same observation taking up multiple rows.

Here’s an example of data that we probably want to pivot wider (unless we want to plot each statistic in its own facet):


# A tibble: 6 × 3
  Group Statistic Value
  <chr> <chr>     <dbl>
1 A     Mean       1.28
2 A     Median     1   
3 A     SD         0.72
4 B     Mean       2.81
5 B     Median     2   
6 B     SD         1.33




A common cue to use pivot_wider() is having measurements of different quantities in the same column.

pivot_wider Example

wide_stats <- long_stats |> 
8  pivot_wider(id_cols = Group,
9              names_from = Statistic,
10              values_from = Value)
wide_stats


8

id_cols is the column that uniquely identifies each row in the new dataset. Default is everything not in names_from and values_from.

9

names_from provides the names that will be used for the new columns

10

values_from provides the values that will be used to populate the cells of the new columns.






# A tibble: 2 × 4
  Group  Mean Median    SD
  <chr> <dbl>  <dbl> <dbl>
1 A      1.28      1  0.72
2 B      2.81      2  1.33



pivot_wider() also has a number of optional names_* and values_* arguments for more complicated transformations.





 Nested Data


If there are multiple rows in the input that correspond to one cell in the output you’ll get a list-column. This means that you 1) need to fix something in your code/data because it shouldn’t be nested in this way or 2) need to use unnest_wider() or unnest_longer() in order to access this column of data. More on this here.

title: Supplemental questions
text:
Questiom 1: Are you applying to this position as an eligible Priority Placement Program Participant who is a current or previous King County Employee impacted by layoff? AND Is this position the same or lower percentage of full-time when compared to the position held at the point of the notification of layoff? AND Do you possess the skills and abilities to qualify for this position?  No
Question 2: If you answered yes to the question above and you are applying for this position as a Priority Placement Participant, to be considered, you must provide the following three pieces of information in the space provided: 1. The title you held when you received your layoff notice 2. The department you worked in 3. The effective date of your layoff.  N/A
Question 3: This is a mid-career data scientist position. Please describe your interest in this position and how it fits into your current career goals.
My interest in this position is the product of a decade spent developing data science skills at a public research university, and a deep desire to utilize those skills in public service. Academic research has taught me to work rigorously with large, messy administrative data such as court records, cancer registries, and vital records, and throughout my tenure I’ve found most meaning in applied work that views social science as public problem-solving: research evidence applied equitably and ethically, alongside the communities it’s meant to serve. Evaluating the H-SYNC pilot program with UW’s CoLab for Community & Behavioral Health Policy, I saw analysis move directly into decisions about a program serving court-involved youth. As a community partner with King County’s Community Resilience + Equity Program, I worked with county staff to translate my research on air pollution and psychological distress toward participatory, community-engaged public-health practice. And as a Data Science for Social Good fellow, I built tools that showed regional transit planners which communities their system underserved — evidence to inform policy that treats transit as a collective good, one that should serve everyone equitably and efficiently. Experiences like these have been the high points of my research career, and as I transition out of formal academic training, I want them to be the foundation of what comes next. That is exactly what draws me to the PME unit’s mandate to turn data into better outcomes for King County residents.
BHRD’s focus makes this position more than a good technical fit. Mental and behavioral health has been a through-line of my research — from first-authored work on air pollution and psychological distress to a dissertation examining how a weight-centered health paradigm shapes behaviors and accompanying mental and physical health outcomes. That focus is not incidental. Mental health and substance use challenges have directly affected my family, friends, and loved ones and I have seen firsthand how much depends on whether care and support are available and accessible when needed. My research has deepened and contextualized that experience: because so many of the conditions that produce these challenges — poverty, housing instability, discrimination, environmental harm — are structural, public programs and policies are among the most powerful levers we have for improving individual and community health. Supporting the crisis continuum with careful, equity-centered analysis is work I would find deeply rewarding.
Finally, this role aligns with where I have most fun working with data and where I want to continue to grow. A consistent thread of my career has been translating complex analyses into insights people can easily understand and act upon: distilling survival models into visual summaries that let H-SYNC program staff and court partners see what the program was achieving; building an interactive dashboard that let transit planners explore millions of trip records intuitively and informatively; and designing visualizations that make complex research findings legible to expert and lay audiences alike. Five years of drop-in consulting at UW’s Center for Social Science Computation and Research — and teaching R to students with no programming background — have taught me to meet any audience at their level of technical comfort and bring them along. A position that asks me to pair advanced analysis with clear, actionable communication, in service of a community I care deeply about, is exactly what I have been looking for - work I want to keep doing and getting better at, for a long time to come.
Question 4: How many years of relevant work experience do you have in social sciences, public health, behavioral health, statistics, finance, or a related field with course work in research methods and statistics?  5+ years of experience
Question 5: King County has transformed its work on equity and social justice from an initiative to an integrated effort that applies the principle of “fair and just” intentionally in all the county does in order to achieve equitable opportunities for all people and communities. Describe how you have placed principles related to equity and social justice at the center of your work and give specific examples if possible. If you do have this experience, please write “N/A”.
Equity and social justice are not simply a component of my work; they are the motivation and lens through which I orient my life. My training as a sociologist is, at its core, training in seeing how systems and structures produce and reproduce inequality — how outcomes that appear individual are actually patterned by place, race, policy, and history. Every research question I have pursued has grown from that understanding. My earliest published work documented how residential segregation concentrates air pollution — and the psychological distress it causes — in communities of color. In Belgium, working with individually linked census and cancer registry records, I measured how socioeconomic position, race, ethnicity, and nativity structure who develops cancer and who survives it, identifying where policy intervention could interrupt those inequities. My dissertation traces how a weight-centered health paradigm with racialized origins shapes health behaviors and outcomes differently by race, sex, class, and neighborhood context. Each of these projects asks, in its own way, where inequity enters and where it can be interrupted.
That orientation matters most precisely where data work looks purely technical, because data is never neutral. Administrative data records the behavior of systems as much as the people in them, and I treat that as an analytic fact with consequences. As a Data Science for Social Good fellow our team quantified who was missing from 21 million transit fare records — riders who pay cash, who are disproportionately low-income — and defined what the data could and could not support, so that a dashboard meant to advance equity would not quietly encode exclusion. In evaluating King County’s H-SYNC pilot, I estimated differential program impacts by race, ethnicity, sex, and geography, and interpreted results in light of how systemic inequities shape both the outcomes youth experience and the administrative records that document them.
The same principles govern my work outside of research. As a teacher, I moved to an ungraded model — built on iterative feedback and student self-assessment — because conventional grading reproduces existing inequities in background and preparation rather than measuring learning. As a community partner with King County’s Community Resilience + Equity Program, I worked with county staff to translate environmental-justice research into participatory, community-engaged practice. Centering equity and social justice is at the heart of my training and the work I’ve done throughout my career, and I am drawn to this position because it offers the chance to keep doing so within a team that has made that orientation its explicit framework.
Question 6: Do you have experience working in health and human services data?  Yes
Question 7: Which statement best describes your professional experience working directly with large administrative healthcare database, and/or utilizing and analyzing data in human services or health care?  5+ years of experience
Question 8: Describe your experience supporting program monitoring and evaluation processes (e.g., development of performance measures, designing an evaluation protocol, collecting data, monitoring program performance, and/or using data to support continuous quality improvement efforts). Please include the organization(s) and dates of employment and highlight evaluation of behavioral health or crisis system programs as relevant. If you do not have this experience, please type N/A.
My most direct evaluation experience comes from my current role with the CoLab for Community & Behavioral Health Policy in UW’s Department of Psychiatry and Behavioral Sciences (January 2024–present), where I partnered with the CoLab Director to design and execute a summative evaluation of the Housing Stability for Youth in Courts (H-SYNC) pilot, a continuum-of-care intervention for court-involved youth at risk of housing instability. I contributed to every stage of the evaluation: defining the evaluation questions, operationalizing outcomes from administrative records, constructing the analytic dataset by extracting and matching individual-level records across two large administrative databases and program service data, building a matched comparison group optimized for covariate balance across 21 demographic and risk metrics, estimating program effects on recidivism through survival analysis, and examining differential impacts by race, ethnicity, sex, and geography. Additionally, the entire analytic pipeline is documented and version-controlled so that the analysis can be rerun and extended as future cohorts complete the program — a summative design constructed to support ongoing monitoring. Throughout, I translated findings into visualizations and written summaries for program staff and system partners.
While H-SYNC was summative rather than ongoing performance monitoring, my broader training and experience map directly onto the monitoring, measurement, and continuous-improvement activities this role involves. My doctoral training is grounded in research methods, study design, and applied statistics, and my research career at UW (Department of Sociology, 2014–2026) has been sustained practice in study design under real-world constraints, measurement operationalization from imperfect instruments, and the iterative cycle of specifying, testing, and refining an analysis. I’m confident these skills transfer readily to developing performance measures, designing data-collection protocols, and building the dashboards and reporting that support continuous quality improvement.
Question 9: Please select the option below that best indicates your level of proficiency using SQL.  Advanced (4+ years of experience)
Question 10: Please select the option below that best indicates your level of proficiency using R.  Advanced (4+ years of experience)
Question 11: Please select the option below that best indicates your level of proficiency using Tableau.  I do not have this experience, but I am willing to learn.

title: Victoria Sass
text:
My research interests broadly focus on health disparities, the social construction of disease, and the relationship between physical and mental health outcomes. My dissertation seeks to better understand the long-term mental and physical health effects of a weight-centric health paradigm through the mechanism of dieting for weight-loss and/or weight-maintenance.

Professional & Research Experience


Jan 2024 – present


CoLab for Community & Behavioral Health Policy  Department of Psychiatry  and Behavioral Sciences, University of WashingtonGraduate Research Assistant,H-SYNC Program Evaluation

Partnered with the Director of CoLab to design and execute a summative evaluation of the Housing Stability for Youth in Courts (H-SYNC) pilot, a continuum of care intervention addressing prevention of housing instability, estimating program impact on youth recidivism to assess whether the intervention’s benefits extended beyond housing into criminal-legal system involvement.
Extracted, cleaned, matched, and validated individual-level records from two large administrative databases — the Assessments Research Database (ARD) and the Court Contact and Recidivism Database (CCRD) — alongside program service data, resolving inconsistent identifiers and coding schemes across systems to construct a unified analytic dataset of youth with documented court contact.
Constructed a matched comparison group from administrative records and implemented a parallelized selection procedure in R to identify the specification achieving optimal covariate balance across 21 key demographic and risk metrics, enabling defensible causal inference from observational data.
Conducted survival analyses in R to estimate the effect of program participation on time to subsequent court contact, and translated findings into visualizations and written summaries for both program staff and system partners without technical backgrounds.
Built a documented, version-controlled (Git/GitHub) analysis pipeline covering ingestion, cleaning, linkage, matching, modeling, and visualization so that the evaluation can be reproduced and extended as additional cohorts complete the program.
Centered equity and social justice in evaluation design by examining differential program impacts across dimensions of sex, race, ethnicity, and geographic context — grounding interpretation in the ways systems inequities shape both the outcomes and the administrative data that record them.
Currently preparing results for publication with the CoLab research team.





Sept 2021 – present


Center for Social Science Computation & Research  University of WashingtonComputational Research and Data Consultant

Serve as technical subject matter expert for students, staff, and faculty across all social science departments, advising on statistical and quantitative software, data management strategy, database access, analytic design, and reproducible research practice through in-person and remote drop-in consulting.
Diagnose and resolve data problems spanning extraction, cleaning, reshaping, merging, and validation, translating researchers’ substantive questions into tractable computational workflows across a wide range of technical sophistication.
Design and teach quarterly R workshops, building instructional materials that make statistical programming accessible to researchers with varying substantive and technical backgrounds.





Jun 2023 - Jun 2026


Doctoral Candidate Department of Sociology,University of Washington

Designed and executed a three-study longitudinal analysis of adolescent-to-adult behavioral, cardiometabolic, and mental health outcomes using restricted-access data from the National Longitudinal Study of Adolescent to Adult Health (N ≈ 15,000; five waves, 1994–2018), conducted entirely within a secure data enclave under strict regulatory and disclosure controls.
Built an end-to-end reproducible pipeline in R, SQL (DuckDB), and Git — from raw multi-wave survey files through harmonized analytic tables, model estimation, and publication-ready output — including custom functions and internal tooling for survey-weighted model tidying, contrast estimation, and automated table/figure generation reused across all three studies.
Constructed longitudinal exposure measures from inconsistently administered, branching survey instruments, resolving item routing, wave-to-wave measurement drift, and item nonresponse across five waves to produce a behavioral trajectory typology used throughout the project.
Developed a supervised machine-learning pipeline (gradient-boosted trees in tidymodels) to detect and correct anomalous measurements across waves, removing investigator discretion from a decision affecting core analytic variables.
Specified and estimated survey-weighted regression models under a complex sampling design, testing mediation, temporal ordering, and effect modification, and quantifying sensitivity to unmeasured confounding and to alternative specifications.
Built a reusable R visualization system (ggplot2, gt, gtsummary) generating marginal-prediction plots, forest plots, and layered model tables, and communicated results through a Quarto/reveal.js presentation designed to make complex, multi-model findings legible to mixed expert and lay audiences.
Documented how exposure to a weight-centered health paradigm with racialized and racist origins produces weight-goal orientations and behavioral repertoires patterned by sex, race, socioeconomic origin, and neighborhood context — and that these orientations and behaviors, rather than body size itself, predict adult health outcomes; currently preparing the studies for publication.





Winter 2024


Multi-State Study of Monetary Sanctions (PI: Alexes Harris) Department of Sociology,University of WashingtonGraduate Research Assistant

Contracted to a multi-state study of court-imposed monetary sanctions and their consequences for low-income defendants, extending Dr. Harris’s A Pound of Flesh: Monetary Sanctions as Punishment for the Poor (2016) to a comparative analysis across eight states.
Cleaned and structured large, unstandardized King County Superior Court administrative records delivered as raw CSV, applying regular expressions and text-as-data methods in R to extract key variables from inconsistently formatted free-text fields.
Executed complex joins across court data sources to build an analysis-ready dataset for the research team, resolving record-matching and structural inconsistencies that had blocked downstream analysis.





Autumn 2015 - Autumn 2021


Demographic Vulnerability, Neighborhood Pollution,Department of Sociology,University of Washingtonand Racial Health Disparities (PI: Kyle Crowder)Graduate Research Assistant

Worked as part of an interdisciplinary team on an NIH-funded study examining how individual, family, and neighborhood social and economic conditions interact with environmental exposure to produce racial and ethnic disparities in health.
Helped build a comprehensive multilevel longitudinal dataset linking micro-level health and life-history records from the Panel Study of Income Dynamics to modeled neighborhood pollution concentrations derived from EPA and other sources, joined to census measures of neighborhood economic and demographic conditions — enabling analysis of exposure histories that follow individuals across residential moves and over time.
Estimated effects of neighborhood pollution exposure on health using longitudinal multilevel models — accounting for confounding and non-random residential selection — and analyzed how racial and ethnic differences in exposure and its amplifying stressors account for persistent racial disparities in health, linking place-, race-, and policy-driven inequities to measurable outcomes.
Co-authored three peer-reviewed publications from the project, including first-authored work establishing air pollution exposure as a driver of individual psychological distress (Health & Place, 2017).





Summer 2020


National Analysis of Rental Markets Using Scraped  College of Built Environments,University of Washington Online Data (PIs: Rebecca Walter and Arthur Acolin)  Summer Graduate Research Assistant

Worked with a multidisciplinary team on an NSF-funded effort to scrape and analyze online rental-listing data across housing markets, contributing to data collection, processing, and analysis for academic papers.
Helped build a visualization dashboard presenting processed data to non-technical community users.
Work resulted in one coauthored publication: “Toward a Cross-Platform Framework: Assessing the Comprehensiveness of Online Rental Listings.” Cityscape (2021).





Autumn 2018 - Spring 2020


Interface Demography Department of Sociology,Vrije Universiteit Brussel (PIs: Sylvie Gadeyne and Harlinde De Schutter)Junior Research Associate (Doctoral Researcher)

Conducted quantitative research on socioeconomic inequalities in site-specific cancer incidence, survival, and mortality as part of a national research team, working with individually linked administrative records connecting the Belgian national census, emigration and mortality registers, and the Belgian Cancer Registry to identify where health inequities emerge and where policy intervention is possible.
Managed, validated, and analyzed highly sensitive population-scale health records under EU data privacy regulation, accessing restricted patient-level cancer registry data through a secure on-site research environment and working within strict governance requirements on disclosure and data handling.
Constructed multidimensional socioeconomic indicators — education, employment, occupation, housing tenure and comfort, household composition — from census records to measure how material, cultural, and social resources structure cancer risk and outcomes across the population.
Applied survival analysis, life table methods, and Poisson regression to estimate absolute and relative inequalities in cancer outcomes by socioeconomic position, decomposing mortality inequalities into their incidence and survival components.
Extended the project’s analytic frame by integrating environmental exposure data, examining how neighborhood environmental quality interacts with socioeconomic position in shaping health outcomes.

In the U.S. visiting family when pandemic travel restrictions took effect in March 2020. Because EU data protection requirements permitted access to the cancer registry records only on-site in Brussels, the work could not continue remotely; I documented and transitioned the analysis to my PIs and redirected my dissertation to a new project.




Summer 2015 - Autumn 2015


Southern Exit-Voice Dynamics in the Jim Crow South  Department of Sociology,University of Washington (PI: Stewart Tolnay)  Graduate Research Assistant

Constructed a multilevel analytic dataset for a study of the relationship between Black out-migration and civil rights protest in the Jim Crow South, integrating four archival and administrative sources — civil rights protest records for 334 southern cities, county-level net-migration estimates, 1960 census data across multiple geographies, and historical records of racial violence — that shared no common identifier and were reported at inconsistent units of analysis.
Audited source data for structural problems prior to linkage, profiling variable distributions and missingness, identifying coverage gaps and cases of multiple cities nested within a single county, and reconstructing county FIPS codes to enable merges across datasets — resolving the join logic and boundary-change issues that made the linkage possible.
Work resulted in one co-authored publication: “Migration and Protest in the Jim Crow South,” Social Science Research (2018).





Summer 2016


Data Science for Social Good Fellow  eScience Institute, University of Washington

Partnered with seven regional transit agencies to establish proof of concept that ORCA electronic fare-payment data — collected for revenue purposes and largely unused for planning since 2009 — could serve as an evidentiary basis for transit system planning and evaluation.
Characterized bias and data quality limitations in a 21-million-record administrative dataset, quantifying the geographic and socio-demographic bias arising from riders who pay in cash and therefore never appear in the data — establishing the conditions under which the data could and could not support valid policy conclusions. Engineered complex joins in SQL across fare-transaction, vehicle-location, and sensor data hosted on a secure AWS RDS instance, constructing unique journey identifiers to reconstruct ~15.5 million multi-agency trips from disconnected boarding records, including inferred alighting locations and transfer behavior.
Developed an R/Shiny application linking ORCA trip records to census tract demographics (race/ethnicity, vehicle access, SNAP participation) enabling regional transit agencies to see which communities the system connected and which it underserved, and foregrounding equity in future planning decisions.
Collaborated with fellows to design the shared architecture integrating four applications into a single coherent dashboard, enabling non-technical planners to visualize, query, and discover patterns across ~7 million origin-destination pairs.

Teaching Experience





Graduate Student Instructor  University of Washington, Seattle
Designed curriculum, developed all course content, and taught quarter-length courses to undergraduate and graduate students across a wide range of substantive and technical backgrounds. Since 2021, designed courses using an ungraded model built on iterative feedback and revision, with students assessing their own learning in one-on-one conferences — keeping attention on learning itself and reducing the ways conventional grading reproduces existing inequities.




Autumn 2025Spring 2024Autumn 2023

The course was designed without prerequisites so it remained accessible and engaging to students with no prior programming experience as well as those seeking to deepen their existing skill set.

Taught classical and contemporary social theory — including conflict, racial formation, feminist, and intersectional frameworks — using an annotation platform for collaborative reading and a peer-reviewed, revised writing sequence, developing students’ ability to construct evidence-based arguments and analyze the social and systemic structures through which power and inequality are produced and reproduced.

Sociology 321: Using Quantitative Data for Social Science Research

text: Below are the steps/rationale taken to clean and merge the individual datasets necessary to conduct our analyses. Code available upon request.

title: Appendix
text:
Data Cleaning

H-SYNC Data

Referral date (from study data) was appended to hsync person file
De-duplicated individuals with two logon-ids in PACT so all PACT data associated with the same logon_id
Linked hsync person-file with PACT data



PACT Data

Merged H-SYNC pact data with non-HSYNC pact data
Recoding date of birth (DOB) variable in PACT (using CCRD DOB)

PACT DOB data was rife with typos resulting in a range in ages of -983.24 to 126.24 years
CCRD DOB data did not have the same issues and had an age range of 9.11 to 48.4 years old
Therefore, PACT DOB was recoded to CCRD DOB (if available) and left as is otherwise

2 non-HSYNC folks had no ccrd DOB and their PACT DOB was erroneous (resulted in ages at PACT assessment start of ~ 0 and 1 year old respectively); they were removed
This resulted in a new PACT DOB range of 9.11 to 48.4 years old
Most common typo errors in PACT dob for year

2995 —> 1995
2202 —> 2002
2204 —> 2004
2033 —> 2003
2012 —> 2002
2013 —> 2003
1983 —> 1993
1984 —> 1994
1885 —> 1995
various iterations of using assessment year (+/- 1 or 2 years) rather than birth year






CCRD Data

Cleaned up zip code variable

In order to derive a county variable (for matching) from the ccrd person-file, the zip code variable was used

Unfortunately this variable had many typos that needed to be handled first

The strategy for the zip code data was:

Use zipcodeR package to create a lookup table of valid zip codes in Washington state
Using said table, separate ccrd person file into observations with:

invalid zip codes (634 obs)

removed missing observations (517 observations)
performed a fuzzy join with zip code lookup table using OSA method and allowing for up to a 2 digit distance from a valid zip code
kept the top 3 matches for each individual by smallest string distance
merged in ccrd disposition data to leverage information about disposition counties

pre-merge, weights for frequency of county appearance were created for each individual (in order to break ties based solely on string distance of potential zip code matches from the fuzzy join)

each potential county match was given a score based upon the product of:

a similarity index of existing zip to potential valid matching zip (using exponential decay function)
1 + 3*county weights from disposition data (if disposition county was not represented in potential fuzzy match this weight = 0)

all county scores within each individual were summed to create a total score for each potential matching county
the highest scoring county was selected for each research id as the most likely county
this resulted in 109 individuals with one most likely county and 27 individuals with tied most likely counties

the tied counties were due to no county match between the fuzzy join counties and ccrd disposition counties
to break the tie, geographic distances between potential fuzzy join counties and all ccrd disposition counties were calculated
the fuzzy join county with the nearest proximity to a ccrd disposition county was selected as the most likely county


valid zip codes

8,915 observations had matching zip codes in the zip code lookup table
11 individuals had multiple valid zip codes, 2 of which had valid zip codes for different counties

county weights from ccrd disposition data were calculated for those 2 individuuals
a weighted score was calculated as (1 + 3*county_weight); again, county_weight = 0 if it was not one of their valid counties from the person-file data
weighted scores were summed by county for each respondent to derive a total score for each valid person-file county
both respondents had a highest total score and were assigned that respective county for the person-file data

the 9 individuals with multiple valid matches that were the same county were deduplicated
all valid observations were combined (8,907 obs)

missing zip codes

first, observations with missing zip codes were anti_joined with all valid zip code observations to remove respondents with a valid observation (2 individuals)
to impute most likely county for the 515 respondents with only missing zip codes, the same ccrd disposition county weights were created

the highest weighted county was assigned as most likely person-file county
there were 7 individuals that had ties for highest county weight but all had birth years outside the range of our treatment group so their first county was selected (they will eventually be filtered out of the data)
6 individuals had no valid zip code and no ccrd disposition data and were removed from the data

509 missing zip codes imputed using ccrd disposition weighted counties




Harmonized multiple logon_ids in CCRD with PACT logon id

27 individuals in ccrd person file data had 2 logon ids

16 were explicit (1 observation per research_id with non-missing values for both logon_id_1 and logon_id_2)
11 were implicit (2 observations per research_id with non_missing value for logon_id_1 and missing for logon_id_2)

these were reformatted to have only 1 observation per person and non-missing values for both logon_id_1 and logon_id_2


An id-lookup table was created from the ccrd person-file using logon_id_1 and logon_id_2

This table was merged with all PACT observations by logon_id_2 thereby adding the associated logon_id_1 value for those with both
logon_id in PACT was recoded to logon_id_1 if non-missing (our duplcates) and kept as is otherwise (everyone else with only one unique logon id value

Merging Data
PACT and CCRD data were then merged into one dataset, with duplicate variables4 from each retained to enable proper recoding.
4 Sex, Race, and County

Recoding Data

Sex

Consistent measures between PACT and CCRD left as is
If CCRD sex missing, assigned PACT sex
Reassigned discrepancies between the two datasets to CCRD sex

Race

Consistent measures between PACT and CCRD left as is
Anything coded as Missing or Other in PACT that had a valid descriptive racial category in CCRD was assigned CCRD race
Values of American in PACT were recoded to their CCRD race

If missing in CCRD, recoded as American Indian/Alaskan Native5

Hispanic was its own racial category in PACT and not part of the racial categories in CCRD (a separate indicator for ethnicity was available)

Anyone coded Hispanic for PACT with a valid racial category in CCRD was assigned their CCRD race
If CCRD race was missing, their race was coded Unknown/Missing

If CCRD race missing, PACT race was assigned
For inconsistencies between PACT and CCRD race, PACT was assigned
If PACT race missing, CCRD was assigned
Multiracial and Other were combined into one category due to small N

Ethnicity (Hispanic)

If CCRD ethnicity was Hispanic, coded Hispanic
If CCRD ethnicity was Not Hispanic but PACT race was Hispanic, coded Hispanic
If CCRD ethnicity was missing, coded Not Hispanic

County

within_county was coded from PACT county
between_county

H-SYNC participants were coded from PACT county
Potential controls were assigned to corresponding PACT county based upon similarity of CCRD counties

King <– King, Spokane, Snohomish6, Pierce7
Kitsap <– Kitsap, Clark, Mason, Jefferson, Clallam
Okanogan <– Okanogan, Kittitas, Ferry, Douglas, Chelan
Snohomish <– Snohomish, Pierce
